## Task: Fix [medium] Security: File-management API exposes browse/read/write/delete with no authentication or authorization
## Step: execute

You are a fix agent. Resolve the following final-review finding.

Review dimension: Security
Severity: medium
File: Controllers/FilesController.cs (all endpoints); Program.cs
Title: File-management API exposes browse/read/write/delete with no authentication or authorization

Finding:
...

Fix instructions (follow exactly; make targeted, minimal changes):
Add an explicit, documented access-control decision for the `/api/files/*` surface in `Controllers/FilesController.cs` and `Program.cs`. Preferred: introduce ASP.NET Core authentication/authorization (e.g., cookie or JWT) — call `builder.Services.AddAuthentication(...).Add...(...)` and `builder.Services.AddAuthorization();`, then `app.UseAuthentication(); app.UseAuthorization();` in the pipeline before `MapControllers()`, and decorate the controller (or each mutating action) with `[Authorize]`. If authentication is intentionally out of scope for this sample app, add a startup guard that refuses to serve the API unless explicitly enabled for local-only use — e.g., when not in Development, require `builder.Configuration["FileService:AllowRemoteAccess"]` to be `"true"` else throw at startup, and/or restrict the endpoints to localhost via a middleware/`UseCors` allow-list of origins. Document the chosen posture in the controller XML doc comment and `README.md` so the open-by-default behavior is not accidentally deployed.

# File Browser

A full-stack file browser with a .NET Web API backend and a TypeScript SPA frontend.

## Prerequisites

- [.NET 10 SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet/10.0)
- [Node.js v20+](https://nodejs.org/)
- npm (ships with Node.js)

## Quick Start

Run the following commands in order from the repository root.

```bash
# 1. Install JavaScript dependencies
npm install

# 2. Build the frontend
#    Compiles TypeScript in src/ to wwwroot/dist/ and copies src/app.css to wwwroot/dist/app.css
npm run build

# 3. Start the backend server
dotnet run --project TestProject.csproj
```

Open the HTTPS URL printed in the console (e.g. `https://localhost:7146`). Click **Browse Files** to open the file-browser dialog.

## Configuration

The server's home directory (where files are stored) is configurable via the `FileService:HomeDirectory` setting.

- **appsettings.json**: set `FileService.HomeDirectory` (default is `"Home"`).
- **Environment variable**: set `FileService__HomeDirectory` (double underscore).

Relative paths resolve under the app content root. The folder is created automatically on first use (lazily, the first time a file-system operation targets it), so a misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at startup.

## Limits

- **Search results are capped at 500 matches.** A recursive search stops collecting once it reaches 500 entries, so very broad queries may return a truncated result set without further indication.
- **Directory copies are capped at 32 levels of nesting.** Copying a tree deeper than 32 levels aborts with an error (`IOException`: "Directory copy depth limit exceeded (possible cycle)") to guard against stack overflow on pathological or self-referential trees.

## Deep Linking

The app keeps its current view and location in the URL hash, so any folder listing or search result can be bookmarked or shared as a direct link.

- `#/browse` — root folder listing.
- `#/browse/Documents/Reports` — a nested folder.
- `#/search?q=budget&path=Documents` — a search scoped to a folder.

Each path segment is percent-encoded (e.g. spaces become `%20`).

## Running Tests

```bash
# Frontend tests (Vitest)
npm test

# Backend tests (xUnit)
dotnet test TestProject.Tests/TestProject.Tests.csproj
```

## Final End-to-End Verification Checklist

1. **`dotnet build TestProject.csproj`** — project compiles without errors.
2. **`npm test`** — all frontend tests pass.
3. **`npm run build`** — succeeds and produces `wwwroot/dist/app.js` and `wwwroot/dist/app.css`.
4. **`dotnet run`** — serves `index.html` at `/` (visit `https://localhost:7146`).
5. **`GET /api/files/browse?path=`** — returns JSON with `entries`, `folderCount`, `fileCount`, and `totalSize`.
6. **UI dialog** — opens when "Browse Files" is clicked.
7. **UI lists files/folders** — entries render in the table with Name, Size, Modified, and Actions columns.
8. **Upload** — file upload works via the Upload button.
9. **Download** — clicking the Download link retrieves the file.
10. **Delete** — Delete action removes files/folders after confirmation.
11. **Move** — Move action renames/relocates entries via a prompt.
12. **Copy** — Copy action duplicates entries via a prompt.
13. **Search** — Search input and button filter entries recursively by name.

<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Exclude the nested xUnit test project so the web app build does not compile test sources. -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="TestProject.Tests/**" />
    <None Remove="TestProject.Tests/**" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- SPA build integration.

       The frontend lives in /src (*.ts) and is compiled to /wwwroot/dist by the
       `npm run build` script (tsc + scripts/copy-assets.mjs). Since wwwroot/dist is
       gitignored, it must be (re)generated before the C# build so the static files
       are present for `UseStaticFiles`/publish. These targets wire npm into the
       MSBuild pipeline so `dotnet build` and `dotnet publish` are self-contained.

       NpmInstall is incremental: it only runs when package.json or package-lock.json
       change (or node_modules is absent). node_modules/.package-lock.json is npm's
       own stamp file and serves as the output/up-to-date marker. -->
  <Target Name="NpmInstall"
          Inputs="package.json;package-lock.json"
          Outputs="node_modules/.package-lock.json">
    <Exec Command="npm install" WorkingDirectory="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- NpmBuild always runs (tsc is fast and honors noEmitOnError), but only after
       deps are in place. Runs before BeforeBuild so dist exists prior to CoreBuild
       and static-asset copy during publish. -->
  <Target Name="NpmBuild"
          DependsOnTargets="NpmInstall"
          BeforeTargets="BeforeBuild">
    <Exec Command="npm run build" WorkingDirectory="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)" />
  </Target>

</Project>

1. **Program.cs** must:
   - Call `AddAuthentication(...)` chained with `.AddCookie(...)` or `.AddJwtBearer(...)` before `builder.Build()`

edits.newText:
## Access Control

The `/api/files/*` surface exposes browse, read, write and delete
operations directly against the server's file system, so **every endpoint
requires authentication and authorization**. The `FilesController` is
decorated with a class-level `[Authorize]` attribute, and the ASP.NET Core
authentication/authorization middleware is wired into the request pipeline in
`Program.cs` (`UseAuthentication()` followed by `UseAuthorization()`, before
the endpoints are mapped). Cookie authentication is the configured scheme,
which is convenient for the SPA frontend because it is served from the same
origin and the browser carries the auth cookie on its `fetch` calls.

**Local development:** because the API now requires an authenticated caller,
requests to `/api/files/*` without a valid auth cookie receive a `401`/redirect
to the login path instead of executing the file operation. If you are
developing against this API and need to exercise it directly (for example via
`curl`), establish a session first through whatever login flow your hosting
environment provides, or adjust the authentication configuration in
`Program.cs` accordingly. Do **not** remove the `[Authorize]` attribute or the
middleware to "fix" a `401` — that re-opens the file-management surface to any
reachable client.

## Limits

Relative paths resolve under the app content root. The folder is created automatically on first use (lazily, the first time a file-system operation targets it), so a misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at startup.

## Access Control

The `/api/files/*` surface exposes browse, read, write and delete
operations directly against the server's file system, so **every endpoint
requires authentication and authorization**. The `FilesController` is
decorated with a class-level `[Authorize]` attribute, and the ASP.NET Core
authentication/authorization middleware is wired into the request pipeline in
`Program.cs` (`UseAuthentication()` followed by `UseAuthorization()`, before
the endpoints are mapped). Cookie authentication is the configured scheme,
which is convenient for the SPA frontend because it is served from the same
origin and the browser carries the auth cookie on its `fetch` calls.

**Local development:** because the API now requires an authenticated caller,
requests to `/api/files/*` without a valid auth cookie receive a `401`/redirect
to the login path instead of executing the file operation. If you are
developing against this API and need to exercise it directly (for example via
`curl`), establish a session first through whatever login flow your hosting
environment provides, or adjust the authentication configuration in
`Program.cs` accordingly. Do **not** remove the `[Authorize]` attribute or the
middleware to "fix" a `401` — that re-opens the file-management surface to any
reachable client.
